root.title: Evenness Alternates for Natural Numbers
root.text:
Theorem. For every natural number n, either n is even or n+1 is even.

Proof. We prove this by induction on n. For n=0, n is even. For the induction
step, assume that either n is even or n+1 is even. We split into cases. If n is
even, then n+1 or n+2 is even because n+2 is even. If n+1 is even, then the
next statement holds because n+1 is even.

children.label: thm:even_or_next_even

Theorem. For every natural number n, either n is even or n+1 is even.

Proof. We prove this by induction on n. For n=0, n is even. For the induction

even, then n+1 or n+2 is even because n+2 is even. If n+1 is even, then the
next statement holds because n+1 is even.

proof.theorem_statement: For every natural number n, either n is even or n+1 is even.

root.text: We prove this by induction on n. For n=0, n is even. For the induction step, assume that either n is even or n+1 is even. We split into cases. If n is even, then n+1 or n+2 is even because n+2 is even. If n+1 is even, then the next statement holds because n+1 is even.
root.goal: For every natural number n, either n is even or n+1 is even.

children.text: Base case n=0: 0 is even, so either 0 is even or 1 is even.
children.goal: Either 0 is even or 0+1 is even.

hints: Base case n=0: 0 is even, so either 0 is even or 1 is even.

children.text: Induction step: assume either n is even or n+1 is even. Split into the case that n is even and the case that n+1 is even.
children.goal: Either n+1 is even or n+2 is even.
hypotheses: Either n is even or n+1 is even.

children.text: Case 1: n is even. Then n=2k for some k, so n+2=2(k+1) is even. Thus either n+1 is even or n+2 is even.
children.goal: Either n+1 is even or n+2 is even.

hints: Case 1: n is even. Then n=2k for some k, so n+2=2(k+1) is even. Thus either n+1 is even or n+2 is even.

children.text: Case 2: n+1 is even. Then the left side of the desired disjunction for n+1 is true.
children.goal: Either n+1 is even or n+2 is even.

hints: Case 2: n+1 is even. Then the left side of the desired disjunction for n+1 is true.

data.split_on: the induction hypothesis: either n is even or n+1 is even
data.exhaustive_reason: The induction hypothesis is exactly a disjunction with these two cases.

notes: The cases handler splits the induction step into the two IH alternatives.

data.principle: ordinary induction on natural numbers starting at 0
induction_hypotheses: Either n is even or n+1 is even.

notes: Classified by explicit phrase: prove this by induction on n.
The induction handler creates one base child and one step child.

data.statement: For every natural number n, either n is even or n+1 is even.
assumptions: n is a natural number, with 0 included
data.conclusion: n is even or n+1 is even
